author: @alexshaw714
text:
Also, the carbon layer, is that activated carbon with binder?
i.e. do I go through the same process as with making the vanadium ink, but just use carbon, DI water, and binder?
Thank you.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: exactly mate

author: @alexshaw714
text:
Hi Rob (or anyone who can answer, as I know Rob is busy),
Another great video, thank you. 
I was wondering about the binder please. Is it just CMC? Or is there another additive in there like SBR? Asking because I'd like to buy the kit, but I'd also like to experiment around it and buying a new kit every time I need some more binder (though I'd be happy to help your work by spending the money), is a bit expensive :)
Any help appreciated.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's CMC/SBR 70/30 by weight mate

replies.author: @alexshaw714
replies.text:
Thanks Rob, much appreciated.
I'm sorry to ask a further question, but is the CMC mixed with DI water first?
When I add the CMC to the SBR directly  I get an 'orrible clumpy mess, but adding the DI water gives me something resembling the binder in your kit, and it's been stable for a week now. I hate to ask another question, but if you could give me a nod on that one I can crack on and experiment :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes mate - you need to mix the CMC first

replies.author: @alexshaw714
replies.text: Thanks Rob. I thought so, I just wanted to be sure. I appreciate you taking the time.

author: @cayrex
text:
The theoretical open voltage of vanadium zinc battery is 2.6V I think (potassium hydroxide electrolyte). Positive: V(OH)5+2 e −→V(OH)5+2 OH−1.36V and negative: Zn+2OH−→Zn(OH)2+2e −1.24V
the oxidation of the zinc is balanced by the reduction of the vanadium. When the battery is charged, the reverse reaction takes place.

author: @stemcampprojects7951
text: I second Alexandru's suggestion.  For a serious home experimenter, the EBD-USB+ is an amazing tool.  It provides a definable load, voltage and amperage measurement, and logging.  There are lots of buying options on eBay for it.  Get the "+" version with the gold top - it handles a wider range.  It doesn't come with cables, but those are readily available, or easy to make.  There are also lots of YouTube videos on how to use it.  You do need a computer to use it - it is useless without one.  I've seen it for under $25 us dollars.

author: @AlexandruBotezatu
text: For the measurement I have been using what Kev Walcroft suggested: EBD-USB Load MTK-PE (can buy it off EBay for something like 30USD). Has given me plenty of insight into how the supercap behaves during discharge, requires a PC to work. Well worth the money spent. Can save the voltage-current graphs for review. Has a constant current discharge mode and also a monitor mode that allows  you to connect a custom load. Also computes mW*h and mA*h values for the discharge.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @thundersack69
replies.text: I recommend the zke ebc-a05 which offers defined Charge and Discharge. The great Advantage is the cycle function. You can easily program it to do hundrets of charge-discharge cycles whithout your presence.

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: One of retrograde steps taken by the U.K. education system, since my school days, was the abolition of Chemistry, Physics and Biology classes, and their replacement with 'Science' classes. Students need to have an appreciation for things in general, but they also need focus. 'Science' is an impossibly nebulous term which includes anything from cookery to rocketry; from finance to gardening. The separate subjects were broad enough, whilst providing avenues within those subjects which a student could explore, often to the benefit of many people. Robert is providing that focus, and pointing out some of the options and often unexplored areas surrounding energy storage devices.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
